supporting_evidence_summary: Normalization organizes data to reduce repeated facts and data anomalies.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: Normalization is a database design technique used to organize data so that repeated facts, update anomalies, insertion anomalies, and deletion anomalies are reduced.
notes: The query deliberately avoids the word normalization.

supporting_evidence_summary: Normalization helps reduce repeated facts and update, insertion, and deletion anomalies.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: Normalization is a database design technique used to organize data so that repeated facts, update anomalies, insertion anomalies, and deletion anomalies are reduced.

supporting_evidence_summary: Definitions and examples of update, insertion, and deletion anomalies.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: An update anomaly happens when the same fact is stored in multiple rows and only some copies are updated. An insertion anomaly occurs when a new fact cannot be stored unless unrelated data is also available. A deletion anomaly happens when deleting one row unintentionally removes another important fact.

supporting_evidence_summary: LEFT JOIN preserves all left-side rows when optional related data such as bookmarks may be absent.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. It is useful when related data is optional. For example, approved resources may be listed even when the current user has not bookmarked them.

supporting_evidence_summary: General definition of LEFT JOIN.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. Missing right-side rows appear as NULL.
notes: Useful secondary evidence but less specific than FX-PDF-002.

supporting_evidence_summary: A junction table resolves many-to-many relationships and improves tag filtering, validation, and reuse.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A junction table resolves a many-to-many relationship. For example, resource_tags can connect resources and tags without storing a comma-separated tag list. This makes tag filtering, tag validation, and tag reuse easier.

supporting_evidence_summary: A junction table connects resources and tags using two foreign keys without comma-separated lists.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: One resource may have many tags. One tag may describe many resources. A junction table connects them without storing comma-separated tag lists. The junction table usually contains two foreign keys.

supporting_evidence_summary: Concise definition and LearnShare example of a junction table.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A junction table connects many-to-many relationships. resource_tags can connect many resources to many tags.

supporting_evidence_summary: A primary key uniquely identifies a row and remains the stable record identity.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A primary key uniquely identifies one row in a table. It should be stable and not reused for a different record.

supporting_evidence_summary: A foreign key stores a related row ID and protects structural relationships.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A foreign key stores the id of a related row in another table. It protects relationships from pointing to missing records.

supporting_evidence_summary: Concise primary-key definition.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A primary key uniquely identifies a row. In many MariaDB designs, this is an integer id column.

supporting_evidence_summary: Concise foreign-key definition and LearnShare example.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A foreign key links one table to another. For example, resources.uploader_account_id can point to accounts.id.

supporting_evidence_summary: Database constraints protect structural integrity, while PHP still enforces workflow, permission, status, and replacement-cycle rules.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: Database constraints protect structural integrity. Workflow rules still belong in PHP application logic. A foreign key can prove that a resource exists, but not that the requester may access it.
ground_truth_locator: Slide 7 — Keys Do Not Replace Workflow Rules

supporting_evidence_summary: CHECK constraints are defense-in-depth and do not replace PHP business-rule validation.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A CHECK constraint restricts allowed values in a row. In this project, CHECK constraints are defense-in-depth. PHP application logic must still enforce business rules.

supporting_evidence_summary: ERDs describe entities and relationships before SQL is written; cardinality helps determine whether foreign keys or junction tables are needed.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: An Entity-Relationship Diagram, or ERD, describes important data objects and how they are related. It helps the project team reason about tables before writing SQL.

supporting_evidence_summary: Foreign keys implement structural links between related records.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A foreign key stores the id of a related row in another table. It protects relationships from pointing to missing records.

supporting_evidence_summary: Joins query related tables through the correct key relationships.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A join combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column.

supporting_evidence_summary: Normalization improves structure, consistency, and maintainability by reducing repeated facts and anomalies.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A normalized design does not automatically mean a perfect design. It is a method for improving structure, consistency, and maintainability.

supporting_evidence_summary: Indexes should be based on actual query needs rather than added randomly.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: An index helps the database find rows faster. It should be based on actual query needs, not added randomly.
notes: Supports refusing to invent one exact universal index plan.

supporting_evidence_summary: Junction tables support tag filtering, validation, and reuse by avoiding comma-separated tag storage.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: This makes tag filtering, tag validation, and tag reuse easier.
notes: The follow-up depends on active-session context from Q-INQ-003.

supporting_evidence_summary: A title can change, while the primary-key ID remains the stable record identity.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: The application should not treat a display title as a reliable primary key. A title can change, but the id remains the record identity.
notes: The follow-up depends on active-session context from Q-INQ-004.

supporting_evidence_summary: Strongly related to ERD and Cardinality Notes through keys, one-to-many, many-to-many, and foreign-key relationships.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: Use keys to identify records and relationships to connect records without duplicating the same fact in many places.

supporting_evidence_summary: Strongly related through normalization, separate entities, keys, and junction-table design.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: Normalization supports clearer table structure by separating entities into tables and linking them through keys.

supporting_evidence_summary: Broad supporting glossary covering keys, foreign keys, and junction tables.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A junction table connects many-to-many relationships. resource_tags can connect many resources to many tags.

supporting_evidence_summary: PDF guidance on LEFT JOIN for optional bookmark data.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. It is useful when related data is optional.
notes: FX-DOCX-001 has relevant optional-relationship content but must be excluded by the explicit PDF filter.

supporting_evidence_summary: PDF guidance showing how a right-table WHERE condition can undermine optional LEFT JOIN behavior.
manually_verified_supporting_passage: With LEFT JOIN, placing a condition on the right table in the WHERE clause can accidentally turn the query into behavior similar to an INNER JOIN.
